vocab.meaning: độ trung thực trong việc tái hiện hình ảnh
vocab.example: Old masters strove for representational fidelity, ensuring every texture and shadow mirrored reality with photographic precision.

vocab.meaning: tính chất phá vỡ các quy tắc/đảo ngược truyền thống
vocab.example: Modern art is often defined by its subversive nature, intentionally challenging the viewer's preconceived notions of beauty.
Classical European art was deeply rooted in mythological themes, depicting gods and legends to convey moral lessons.

vocab.meaning: các chất liệu/phương tiện không truyền thống
vocab.example: While old art relied on oil and stone, modern creators often utilize unconventional media such as industrial waste or digital code.
Traditional landscape painters used atmospheric perspective to create an immense sense of spatial depth on a flat canvas.
In contrast to the ornate details of the Baroque era, much of modern art embraces a minimalist aesthetic where less is more.
Old artworks are often valued for their historical provenance, providing a window into the socio-political climate of past centuries.

vocab.meaning: nghệ thuật sắp đặt có tính tương tác
vocab.example: A hallmark of the modern era is the interactive installation, where the audience becomes an integral part of the artistic experience.

vocab.meaning: vẻ đẹp lý tưởng hóa (hoàn mỹ hơn thực tế)
vocab.example: Ancient Greek sculptures were celebrated for portraying idealized beauty, focusing on perfect proportions and athletic forms.

vocab.meaning: phá vỡ/vượt qua các giới hạn
vocab.example: Modern art is characterized by its boundary-pushing approach, constantly redefining the definition of what art can actually be.
